title: Quiz: Variational Quantum Classifiers
description: Test your understanding of VQC architecture, training, and applications

questions.question: What are the three main components of a Variational Quantum Classifier (VQC)?

options.text: Data encoding S(x), variational circuit W(theta), and measurement
Input layer, hidden layer, output layer

questions.explanation: A VQC consists of: (1) Data encoding S(x) that embeds classical data into quantum states, (2) Variational circuit W(theta) with trainable parameters, and (3) Measurement to extract classical predictions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of data re-uploading in VQC?

options.text: To reduce the number of qubits needed
To increase circuit depth without adding parameters
To increase expressibility by encoding data multiple times interleaved with variational layers

questions.explanation: Data re-uploading interleaves data encoding with variational layers multiple times. This significantly increases the model's expressibility and ability to learn complex decision boundaries without adding more qubits.

questions.question: In binary classification with VQC, how is the class prediction typically obtained?

options.text: By measuring all qubits and counting zeros
By computing the expectation value of Z on designated qubit(s) and thresholding

questions.explanation: For binary classification, we typically measure the expectation value <Z> on one or more qubits. The value ranges from -1 to +1, which is mapped to class probabilities. A threshold (usually 0) determines the predicted class.

questions.question: Why is SPSA often preferred over standard gradient descent for training VQC on real hardware?

options.text: It requires only 2 function evaluations per iteration regardless of parameter count
It guarantees finding the global minimum

questions.explanation: SPSA estimates the gradient using only 2 objective function evaluations per iteration, independent of the number of parameters. This is much more efficient than parameter-shift (2 evaluations per parameter) when running on noisy quantum hardware.

questions.question: In angle encoding, the dimension of the feature space equals the number of qubits.
questions.explanation: False. While angle encoding uses n qubits to encode n features, the quantum state lives in a 2^n dimensional Hilbert space. This exponential scaling is a key advantage of quantum feature spaces.

questions.question: What entanglement pattern creates the most connections but has highest gate count?
questions.explanation: Full entanglement connects every qubit pair with CNOT gates, giving O(n^2) gates per layer. This maximizes entanglement but increases circuit depth and noise. Linear uses O(n) gates, circular adds one extra gate to linear.

questions.question: For multi-class classification with k classes, how many measurement qubits are typically needed?
questions.explanation: We need ceil(log2(k)) measurement qubits to encode k classes, since m qubits can represent 2^m different measurement outcomes. For example, 3 qubits can encode up to 8 classes.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid loss functions for VQC training? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: BCE, MSE, and hinge loss are all valid classification losses. BCE is standard for probability outputs, MSE works for regression-style training, and hinge loss gives SVM-style margins. Ground state energy is for VQE, not classification.
